terms.definition: Software that automatically displays or downloads advertising material, often bundled with free programs. Some adware tracks browsing habits to serve targeted ads.
A social engineering attack in which a physical device — such as a USB drive — is deliberately left in a public location, hoping the target will plug it into their machine and execute malicious code.
A network of compromised computers controlled remotely by an attacker, typically used to launch DDoS attacks, send spam, or mine cryptocurrency without the owners' knowledge.
A trial-and-error method of cracking passwords by systematically trying every possible combination until the correct one is found.
A form of business email compromise where an attacker impersonates a senior executive to trick employees into transferring funds, sharing sensitive data, or bypassing normal approval processes.
The infrastructure and communication channels an attacker uses to maintain contact with and send instructions to compromised systems inside a target network.
An automated attack that uses large sets of stolen username-password pairs from previous data breaches to attempt logins on other services, exploiting password reuse.
The unauthorized use of a victim's computing resources to mine cryptocurrency, through malicious scripts embedded in websites or malware installed on the device.
An incident in which sensitive, protected, or confidential data is accessed, copied, transmitted, or stolen by an unauthorized party.
An attack that overwhelms a server, service, or network with a flood of internet traffic from multiple compromised sources, making it unavailable to legitimate users.
A password-cracking technique that tries words from a precompiled list of common passwords and dictionary words rather than every possible combination.
An attack that corrupts Domain Name System data so that a domain name resolves to an incorrect IP address, silently redirecting users to a malicious site.
Taking control of a domain name from its rightful owner by exploiting vulnerabilities in the registrar's systems or through social engineering of support staff.
The process of converting readable data (plaintext) into an unreadable format (ciphertext) using an algorithm and a key, so only authorized parties can access the original information.
A communication system where only the sender and intended recipient can read the messages. No intermediary, including the service provider, can access the data.
A piece of code, software, or technique that takes advantage of a vulnerability in a system to cause unintended behavior, such as gaining unauthorized access.
A network security device or software that monitors and filters incoming and outgoing traffic based on predefined rules.
A system that monitors network traffic or host activity for suspicious behavior or known attack signatures and generates alerts when potential threats are detected.
A social engineering technique where the attacker pretends to be a trusted individual — such as a coworker, IT technician, or authority figure — to manipulate the victim.
The organized approach to detecting, containing, eradicating, and recovering from a cybersecurity incident.
Surveillance software or hardware that records every keystroke made on a device, often used by attackers to capture passwords, credit card numbers, and other sensitive information.
A catch-all term for any software intentionally designed to cause damage, steal data, or gain unauthorized access. Includes viruses, worms, trojans, ransomware, spyware, and adware.
A security mechanism that requires users to provide two or more independent verification factors before granting access to an account or system.
An attack where the adversary secretly intercepts and potentially alters communication between two parties who believe they are communicating directly.
An attack that tries a small number of commonly used passwords against a large number of accounts, avoiding lockout policies that trigger from repeated attempts on a single account.
A software update released by a vendor to fix security vulnerabilities, bugs, or other issues. Timely patching is one of the most effective defenses against known exploits.
A cyber attack that redirects website traffic from a legitimate site to a fraudulent one by exploiting DNS vulnerabilities, without requiring the victim to click a malicious link.
A fraudulent attempt to obtain sensitive information — such as usernames, passwords, or credit card details — by disguising as a trustworthy entity in electronic communication, most commonly email.
Measures designed to prevent unauthorized physical access to facilities, equipment, and resources. Includes locked server rooms, badge access, surveillance cameras, and visitor sign-in policies.
A social engineering technique where the attacker creates a fabricated scenario (a pretext) to engage a victim and build enough trust to extract confidential information.
Malware that encrypts a victim's files or locks them out of their system, then demands a ransom payment — usually in cryptocurrency — in exchange for the decryption key.
Malware that gives an attacker full remote control over a victim's computer, including access to files, webcam, microphone, and keystrokes, often without visible indication.
A warning sign or indicator that something may be suspicious, fraudulent, or malicious. Learning to recognize red flags in emails, messages, and conversations is a core cybersecurity skill.
An attack where an adversary takes over an active user session by stealing or predicting a valid session token, allowing them to impersonate the user.
A system that collects, aggregates, and analyzes log data from across an organization's IT infrastructure to detect anomalies and support incident response.
A phishing attack delivered via SMS text messages. The message typically contains a link to a fraudulent website or a phone number connecting to an attacker.
The psychological manipulation of people into performing actions or divulging confidential information. Exploits human trust, fear, urgency, and helpfulness rather than technical vulnerabilities.
A targeted phishing attack directed at a specific individual or organization, using personalized information to increase credibility and likelihood of success.
Forging identifying information — such as an email address, phone number, IP address, or URL — to disguise the true origin of a communication.
Malicious software that secretly monitors and collects information about a user's activities — including browsing habits, keystrokes, and personal data — and transmits it to a third party.
Cryptographic protocols that encrypt data transmitted between a web browser and a server, indicated by the padlock icon and 'https' in the URL bar.
An authentication method allowing a user to log in once with a single set of credentials and gain access to multiple related applications without re-entering their password.
A physical social engineering technique where an unauthorized person follows an authorized individual through a secured entrance without using their own credentials.
Malware disguised as legitimate software that gives an attacker unauthorized access once installed. Unlike viruses, trojans do not self-replicate — they rely on the user to install them.
Registering domain names that are common misspellings of popular websites to capture traffic from users who mistype URLs, often redirecting them to phishing pages.
Voice phishing — a social engineering attack conducted over the phone. The caller impersonates a trusted entity to pressure the victim into revealing sensitive information.
A service that creates an encrypted tunnel between your device and the internet, masking your IP address and protecting your data from interception.
A weakness or flaw in a system's design, implementation, or configuration that could be exploited by an attacker.
A highly targeted spear phishing attack aimed at senior executives, crafting personalized messages to trick targets into authorizing large transactions or disclosing sensitive corporate data.
A web security vulnerability that allows an attacker to inject malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users, potentially stealing session cookies or redirecting users.
A software vulnerability unknown to the vendor and for which no patch exists yet. Particularly dangerous because defenders have had zero days to prepare a fix.
